CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and states its main contributions early (combining compositional methods with embeddings, the APT approach, and implications for smoothing), and the methodology and experiments follow a logical introduction→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and the description of composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI) and datasets is clear; figures/tables and examples are reported as informative and writing quality is generally adequate. Minor presentation issues remain: some preprocessing and design choices (e.g. recombining 90 compounds, ignoring certain dependency paths, and the handling of shifted PMI) would benefit from more formal justification or ablation to avoid reader confusion, and the reliance on a small 90-item evaluation set is noted without extensive discussion of its implications. | B(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and consistently (combining compositional methods with low-dimensional embeddings; APT approach; smoothing strategy). Notation and methodology are presented with sufficient detail to reproduce experiments (dependencies preprocessed, composition ops ⊔INT and ⊔UNI, hyperparameters reported), and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is adequate, though a few methodological choices (e.g., preprocessing that ignores certain dependency paths, and the impact of shifted PMI) could be explained more clearly and figures/tables are not clearly described in the summary, so minor clarifications would improve readability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and states its main contributions early (combining compositional methods with embeddings, the APT approach, and implications for smoothing), and the methodology and experiments follow a logical introduction→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and the description of composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI) and datasets is clear; figures/tables and examples are reported as informative and writing quality is generally adequate. Minor presentation issues remain: some preprocessing and design choices (e.g. recombining 90 compounds, ignoring certain dependency paths, and the handling of shifted PMI) would benefit from more formal justification or ablation to avoid reader confusion, and the reliance on a small 90-item evaluation set is noted without extensive discussion of its implications.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and consistently (combining compositional methods with low-dimensional embeddings; APT approach; smoothing strategy). Notation and methodology are presented with sufficient detail to reproduce experiments (dependencies preprocessed, composition ops ⊔INT and ⊔UNI, hyperparameters reported), and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is adequate, though a few methodological choices (e.g., preprocessing that ignores certain dependency paths, and the impact of shifted PMI) could be explained more clearly and figures/tables are not clearly described in the summary, so minor clarifications would improve readability.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful empirical contribution with reasonable methodology and candid discussion of limitations, but is constrained by a very small single dataset, limited generalization across phrase types/corpora, and some reproducibility/theoretical gaps (no released code, scant statistical testing). Given the presence of sensible ablations and thorough related work but insufficient evidence to fully support broad claims, a middle score reflects that the work is promising yet needs more extensive evaluation and stronger validation to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is limited by a very small target set (90 compounds), which is well below my threshold for robust assessment of compositionality claims, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing to support reported improvements. While the paper compares multiple composition operations (⊔INT, ⊔UNI), reports hyperparameters, and discusses some negative results (e.g. shifted PMI harms performance) and preprocessing choices, it lacks broader datasets, formal analysis of preprocessing recombination choices, and thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses tied to those design decisions. Related work coverage appears thorough, but reproduci­bility is borderline (code not mentioned) and important theoretical justifications for modeling choices are missing, so the evidence is insufficient to fully support the strong claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid and well-targeted: they compare APT sparse representations and neural word2vec embeddings, evaluate multiple composition operators (⊔INT and ⊔UNI), report hyperparameters, and measure correlation with human judgments on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset—supporting the main claims. Ablations/sensitivity analyses are present (e.g., testing shifted PMI effects and different composition strategies), though evaluation is limited to a single small dataset and one phrase type, leaving generalization to other corpora and phrase classes underexplored. Related work appears thorough and negative results/limitations (e.g., PMI effects, sparse representation issues, preprocessing choices) are candidly discussed. Reproducibility is reasonably supported by methodological detail and reported hyperparameters, but absence of released code and reliance on bespoke preprocessing reduce ease of exact replication.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work presents a useful and practically relevant integration of APT-style structured compositional methods with low-dimensional embeddings, yielding empirical gains and offering methodological extensions (e.g., treating compounds as single lexical items and smoothing/diagnostic strategies). While the contribution is incremental rather than a major theoretical breakthrough, its non-trivial methodological innovations and potential to inform follow-up work justify a slightly above-mid originality score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a moderately novel integration: applying the APT (Anchored Packed Trees) framework to compositionality detection and combining traditional, syntax-aware compositional methods with low-dimensional embeddings, which is a meaningful extension beyond simple neural addition baselines (e.g., the Reddy baseline). However, the core ideas are largely combinations and adaptations of existing techniques (APT, pointwise/intersective composition, word2vec), rather than a wholly new formalism or algorithmic breakthrough. The authors do differentiate their contributions from prior work and make useful empirical claims (e.g., about smoothing and the benefit of maintaining syntactic structure), but the novelty is principally an informed synthesis and application rather than a fundamental conceptual advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and practically useful hybrid perspective by combining traditional structured compositional methods (APT) with low-dimensional neural embeddings to improve non-compositional phrase detection — a clear differentiation from prior work that mostly applied simple vector addition or untyped composition. Recasting compound nouns as single lexical items in ukWaC and evaluating intersective vs. additive composition methods on the Reddy et al. dataset is a non-trivial methodological extension that preserves syntactic structure in contextual features and yields empirical gains. While the core problem (compositionality detection) is well-studied, the integrated approach and the proposal to use APTs for fine-grained diagnostics and smoothing strategies is likely to inform experts and spur follow-up work.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper reports key high-level details (datasets, hyperparameters, composition operations) making reimplementation feasible in principle, but code is not released and several low-level preprocessing and training choices (parser/tokenization, exact recombination, shifted PMI/word2vec settings, APT construction) are under-specified. Given these omissions and the small 90-item evaluation that may leave parameter choices underconstrained, faithful replication would require contacting the authors or making nontrivial assumptions, so I mark replicability as moderate (3/5).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and composition variants and uses publicly available corpora (ukWaC) and the Reddy et al. (2011) 90‑item dataset, and describes the preprocessing step of recombining the 90 target compounds; these facts make reimplementation possible in principle. However, key reproducibility gaps remain: code is not provided, some preprocessing choices (e.g. ignoring dependency paths that include the modifier, exact recombination procedure) and the treatment of low‑frequency items/shifted PMI are only qualitatively discussed, and the small 90‑item evaluation means parameter choices may be underconstrained. From a formalist standpoint these omissions (and lack of full preprocessing ablation) prevent full, reliable replication without contacting the authors.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and composition operations (⊔INT as pointwise min, ⊔UNI with parameter h) and uses publicly-known corpora (ukWaC) and the Reddy et al. (2011) 90-item dataset, and describes the preprocessing step of recombining target compounds in a dependency-parsed ukWaC, which makes reimplementation feasible. However, code is not provided and some implementation details are under-specified in the summary (e.g., exact parser/tokenization choices, precise training regime for word2vec/shifted PMI settings, and low-level APT construction choices), so a faithful reproduction would require minor assumptions or contacting authors.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant problem for an NLP/ML venue and uses appropriate hybrid APT/neural methodology with clear empirical gains. I score it a 4 because, while the work is promising and of clear interest, the concerns about limited dataset size, lack of formal justification for the composition operators, and missing significance/ablation analyses justify reserving top score until those evaluation and analysis weaknesses are addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (compositionality detection with distributional and APT methods) is squarely within NLP/ML venue scope and the methodology (comparison of typed sparse APTs and neural embeddings, ukWaC corpus, and Reddy et al. judgments) will interest the community. However, from a theoretical-formalist viewpoint the paper leans heavily on empirical tweaks without a formal account of why the proposed composition operators should capture non-compositionality, evaluates on only 90 target compounds (well below my 200-item threshold), and the summary does not report statistical-significance testing or ablations for preprocessing choices (e.g. recombining constituents, ignoring certain dependency paths). Given these concerns the work is borderline appropriate: relevant and promising but needing stronger formal grounding and larger/more rigorous evaluation to be a clear fit for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core distributional-semantics problem (non-compositional phrase detection) using a hybrid of symbolic (APT) and neural (word2vec) methods and demonstrates empirical gains over the Reddy et al. baseline (reported correlations up to 0.74). The methodology and evaluation (ukWaC preprocessing, Reddy et al. 90-item judgments, clear baselines and hyperparameter reporting) match community expectations for reproducible, empirical work, and the focus on interpretable diagnostics (APT, compositionality vs similarity) targets the right audience. While the dataset is modest and code release is not mentioned, those limitations are acknowledged and do not outweigh the paper’s relevance to the venue.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper clearly states its claims and presents plausible experimental gains from combining APT-style composition with embeddings, but they also concur on important methodological limitations: the very small 90-item evaluation, recombining targets into the corpus (potential processing bias), and absence of statistical significance testing or formal analyses. These issues meaningfully weaken confidence in the generality and robustness of the conclusions, so a cautious, provisional score is warranted pending larger-scale evaluation and firmer statistical/formal validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and provides experimental evidence that combining APT-style compositional methods with low-dimensional embeddings improves non-compositionality detection, but the evaluation is small-scale (only 90 compound nouns) and thus insufficient for strong generalization. No formal derivations or proofs for the proposed integrated compositional model are reported, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which weakens confidence in the reported improvements. The preprocessing choice to recombine the 90 target compounds into single lexical items and the omission of certain dependency paths are not formally justified or ablated, creating potential logical gaps and possible information leakage. Given these methodological limitations the conclusions are provisionally supported but require larger-scale evaluation and firmer statistical and formal analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing gains when combining APT-style structured composition with low-dimensional embeddings on the Reddy et al. (2011) 90-item dataset, and hyperparameters and baselines (including the REDDY additive baseline) are reported. Mathematical aspects appear straightforward (composition operators and similarity comparisons) and no obvious derivation errors are evident from the summary. Concerns that prevent a perfect score: the experimental set is small (90 compounds), targets were recombined into the corpus which could introduce corpus-processing bias, and no mention of statistical significance testing or code release limits reproducibility. Overall the results and conclusions are plausible and mostly well-supported, with only minor methodological weaknesses.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes fair, direct comparisons to the key Reddy et al. baseline using the same dataset and metrics and provides a thoughtful analysis of sparse vs. dense composition. However, they also concur that the evaluation is narrowly focused (the small 90-item Reddy set), lacks broader contemporary baselines and larger-scale validation or statistical testing, which limits the strength of comparative claims. Given these substantive limitations, a middle score reflecting reasonable but incomplete comparison is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares directly to the principal prior baseline (Reddy et al., 2011) and evaluates under the same human-judgment metric on the same Reddy dataset, and it clearly analyzes how APT-based composition differs from simple composition of neural embeddings. However, the empirical comparison is narrowly focused (only the 90-item Reddy set) and does not include a broader set of contemporary neural or compositional baselines; statistical testing and larger-scale validation are absent, which weakens claims of superiority. The authors do acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably and provide thoughtful discussion of related approaches, but the limited baseline breadth and small evaluation size prevent a fully convincing comparative claim.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a key baseline (Reddy et al. 2011) and contrasts sparse APT representations with neural word2vec compositions using the same ukWaC preprocessing and the Reddy et al. 90-phrase evaluation, so comparisons are largely fair and use the same metrics. The authors report hyperparameters, analyze when sparse versus dense methods help (and note limitations such as dataset size and shifted-PMI effects), and acknowledge when prior methods are competitive. I dock one point because broader baselines (e.g., more recent neural compositional models or evaluations on additional datasets/phrase types) are not included, though related work coverage is reported as thorough.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and demonstrates that combining structured APT representations with embeddings improves detection of non-compositional phrases versus strong baselines. The main limitation is the small evaluation set and some missing statistical/formal details, which temper immediate impact but do not undermine the clear empirical gains and practical promise; hence a score slightly above midpoint reflects solid contribution with room for broader validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful problem—detecting non-compositional phrases—and offers a theoretically motivated integration (APT) that preserves syntactic structure and demonstrably improves over simple neural composition on the ukWaC + word2vec setup. However, from my formalist standpoint the empirical support is limited: evaluation uses only 90 compounds (well below my 200-item threshold) and the summary does not report clear statistical testing or formal analyses justifying preprocessing choices (e.g. recombining lexical items). Thus the contribution is a useful, theoretically interesting step that could influence follow-up work, but its immediate impact is moderate until larger-scale, statistically rigorous evaluations and clearer formal characterizations are provided.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—detecting non-compositional phrases—and provides empirically-backed evidence that hybridizing structured compositional methods (APT) with low-dimensional embeddings improves performance over common baselines (e.g., Reddy et al.) on a standard dataset (reported correlation ≈0.74). From my perspective as an empirical systems researcher, the work is impactful because it both offers interpretable diagnostics (distinguishing compositionality from similarity) and proposes concrete downstream implications (smoothing strategies based on non-compositionality) that practitioners can adopt. While the dataset is small (90 compounds) and code availability is unclear, the clear ablations, comparison to strong baselines, and the promise of APTs to guide further work make this a strong contribution likely to influence follow-up research.

main_claims: Combining traditional compositional methods with low-dimensional word embeddings significantly improves detection of non-compositional compound nouns.
An integrated compositional distributional approach that maintains syntactic structure within contextual features yields further improvements over applying simple composition to neural embeddings.

The APT (Anchored Packed Trees) approach to compositionality is promising for fine-grained understanding and for evaluating compositional methods without conflating phrase similarity and compositionality judgments.

Detecting non-compositional phrases can inform improved smoothing strategies for distributional representations (e.g., smoothing observed phrasal representations instead of constituents when phrases are non-compositional).
_summary.methodology: They preprocess a dependency-parsed ukWaC corpus to recombine 90 target compound nouns into single lexical items, build elementary APT (typed, sparse) representations and neural word2vec embeddings, and compare composition operations (⊔INT = pointwise min and ⊔UNI = weighted addition with parameter h) to infer phrasal representations and detect compositionality by comparing composed vs observed phrase vectors.
datasets: Reddy et al. (2011) dataset of 90 compound nouns with human compositionality judgments
baselines: REDDY baseline (Reddy et al., 2011: weighted addition of untyped co-occurrence vectors)
metrics: Correlation with human judgments of compositionality (reported as performance, e.g., 0.74)
limitations: Further work needed with other datasets and other types of phrase (stated).
Shifted PMI (analogous to negative sampling) had a strong negative effect in their experiments, likely due to low frequency of many compound nouns.
Sparse elementary representations can hurt intersective composition methods; dependency paths including the modifying constituent are ignored in preprocessing (design choice).

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Formalist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a focus on formal semantics and compositionality theory; longtime reviewer for ACL and TACL.
traits: penalises papers that rely primarily on empirical experiments without providing formal definitions or theoretical analysis of the proposed method
penalises papers that evaluate on fewer than 200 target items (treats small-scale evaluation like 90 phrases as insufficient)
requires clear statistical significance testing for claimed improvements (will deduct points if tests are absent or incomplete)
penalises when preprocessing choices (e.g., recombining lexical items) are not justified with formal criteria or ablation
favors explicit modeling of linguistic structure over purely empirical heuristics and will downscore papers that present incremental empirical tweaks to established methods
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal clarity and theoretical contribution first, treats empirical results as supportive evidence only, and deducts points for small-scale evaluations and missing statistical rigor.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Systems Champion
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher specializing in distributional semantics and practical representation learning; frequent reviewer and area chair for EMNLP/NAACL with emphasis on reproducible system improvements.
traits: rewards papers that combine symbolic (structured) methods with neural embeddings and demonstrate empirical improvements over strong baselines
rewards practical advances that improve a downstreamly-relevant metric even on modest datasets (will not penalize evaluations smaller than 200 items if methods are well described and reproducible)
gives credit for interpretable or diagnostically useful approaches (e.g., methods that help distinguish compositionality from similarity)

prefers experiments that include clear ablations and comparisons to common baselines (e.g., word2vec, additive composition); will upscore papers that release code/data or give sufficient details to reproduce
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes empirical gains, reproducibility, and practical usefulness; rewards hybrid methods and interpretable diagnostics even when evaluated on modestly-sized curated datasets.
